title: Sudden fatigue and dark urine after starting a new medication

chart.oneLiner: 28M of Mediterranean descent presents with two days of fatigue, jaundice, and dark (cola-colored) urine that began shortly after starting a new antibiotic for a urinary tract infection.

opening: I've felt wiped out and short of breath for a couple of days, my eyes look a bit yellow, and my urine has turned almost like cola.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 28-year-old man of Mediterranean (Greek) descent, mildly jaundiced and tired-appearing. Cooperative, mildly anxious about the dark urine.

HPI: Acute onset over 48 hours of fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and lightheadedness. Noticed scleral yellowing and dark cola-colored urine. Symptoms started two to three days after beginning a new antibiotic for a urinary tract infection (only volunteer the antibiotic if asked about recent medications/new drugs). Mild back/flank discomfort with the dark urine. NO frank red blood in urine, NO dysuria now, NO black/bloody stools, NO trauma. Reports a similar but milder episode years ago after eating fava beans, which he otherwise avoids (only if asked about prior episodes or diet/food triggers).
PMH: otherwise healthy; episode of neonatal jaundice per his mother (only if asked). A maternal uncle has 'a blood problem with certain drugs' (family history, only if asked). MEDS: recently started antibiotic for UTI — when asked, identify it as a sulfa antibiotic (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole). No regular medications. ALLERGIES: none known. SOCIAL: occasional alcohol; non-smoker; no IV drugs; recently ate at a relative's home (fava beans served — only if asked about diet).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tired-appearing, mildly jaundiced, not in distress.
- Vitals: BP 122/74, HR 108, RR 18, 37.2°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- HEENT: scleral icterus; conjunctival pallor.
- Abdomen: soft; spleen tip only minimally palpable / generally not enlarged; no marked tenderness.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur. No petechiae/purpura; no focal neuro deficit.
- Skin: no petechiae, no purpura, no rash.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the new antibiotic, the fava bean exposure, the neonatal jaundice, or the family history unless asked about recent medications, diet/food, prior episodes, or family history respectively.
- The spleen finding is revealed only if the abdomen/spleen is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: Hgb 8.4, MCV 88 (normocytic), WBC normal, Plt 265 (NORMAL platelets — important to exclude TTP/HUS).
labs.Reticulocyte: Elevated reticulocyte count (appropriate marrow response to hemolysis).
labs.Smear: Bite cells and blister cells; with supravital stain Heinz bodies present; NO schistocytes (argues against microangiopathy); polychromasia.
labs.Hemolysis: LDH HIGH, indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin HIGH, haptoglobin LOW, plasma/urine free hemoglobin present — an intravascular hemolytic pattern.
labs.Coombs: Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test NEGATIVE — argues against autoimmune hemolysis.
labs.G6PD: G6PD enzyme activity LOW once the acute episode/reticulocytosis settles (may be falsely normal during the acute crisis because young reticulocytes are enzyme-replete — repeat in 2–3 months).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemic changes (compensatory response to anemia).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemic changes. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: No acute cardiopulmonary process; clear lungs with normal cardiac silhouette.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: normal chest radiograph with no acute process. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this acute anemia (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute hemolytic anemia — oxidative/drug- or food-triggered (G6PD deficiency) (leading,7). Microangiopathic hemolysis — TTP/HUS (CANT-MISS to exclude,5). Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (core,3). Drug-induced hemolysis (other mechanisms) (core,3). Hemoglobinuria from other causes (e.g., rhabdomyolysis, transfusion) (core,2). Names hemolysis and the imperative to look for schistocytes/thrombocytopenia to exclude TTP/HUS (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute hemolytic anemia from oxidative stress (G6PD deficiency) triggered by a new drug/fava beans. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Microangiopathic hemolysis — TTP/HUS. Also reasonable: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia; Drug-induced hemolysis by other mechanisms; Other causes of hemoglobinuria.

criticalActions.item: Acute hemolytic anemia — oxidative/drug- or food-triggered (G6PD deficiency)

coreActions.item: Hemoglobinuria from other causes (rhabdomyolysis, transfusion reaction)
Names hemolysis and the imperative to look for schistocytes/thrombocytopenia to exclude TTP/HUS

acceptableAlternatives: Hemoglobinuria from other causes (rhabdomyolysis, transfusion reaction)

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CBC + reticulocyte count (CANT-MISS,3). Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes (CANT-MISS,4). Hemolysis labs — LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin, urine hemoglobin (2). Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test — expected NEGATIVE (CANT-MISS,3). G6PD enzyme assay (confirmatory, repeat after acute episode) plus renal function/platelets to exclude TTP/HUS (3). PENALTY: relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC + reticulocyte count; Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells and Heinz bodies vs schistocytes; Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test; G6PD enzyme assay (with awareness it can be falsely normal during the acute episode and should be repeated in 2–3 months).

criticalActions.item: Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes

coreActions.item: G6PD enzyme assay (repeat after acute episode) plus platelets/renal function to exclude TTP/HUS

penalties.item: relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis

requiredTests: Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes
optionalTests: G6PD enzyme assay (repeat after acute episode) plus platelets/renal function to exclude TTP/HUS
contraindicatedTests: relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis
orderSensitiveActions: relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); no acute ischemic changes (compensatory tachycardia from anemia) (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: no acute ischemic ST/T changes (compensatory for anemia)
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia; no acute ischemic changes.
commonMistakes: over-calling ischemia from rate-related ST/T changes
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemic changes — a compensatory response to anemia.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia; no acute ischemic changes.
coreActions: no acute ischemic ST/T changes (compensatory for anemia)

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes it helps exclude an alternate cause of dyspnea (pneumonia, effusion) (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes it excludes pneumonia/effusion as a cause of dyspnea
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate or effusion that is not present
steps.idealAnswer: No acute cardiopulmonary process; clear lungs and normal cardiac silhouette, excluding pneumonia or effusion as the cause of dyspnea.

coreActions: notes it excludes pneumonia/effusion as a cause of dyspnea

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Reticulocytosis, low haptoglobin, high LDH/indirect bilirubin, bite cells and Heinz bodies, NO schistocytes, normal platelets, a NEGATIVE Coombs, and a recent oxidant drug/fava bean exposure. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: G6PD deficiency with acute oxidative hemolysis (6). Cites bite cells/Heinz bodies with a NEGATIVE DAT and an oxidant trigger; explicitly rules out TTP/HUS via the absence of schistocytes, normal platelets, and normal renal function, and rules out autoimmune hemolysis via the negative Coombs (4).
steps.idealAnswer: G6PD deficiency causing acute oxidative (drug/fava-bean triggered) hemolysis.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH hemolytic anemia: immediately STOP/avoid the offending oxidant drug and counsel on triggers (CANT-MISS,7); supportive care with hydration to protect kidneys from hemoglobinuria and monitor Hgb/renal function (4); transfuse if symptomatic/severe anemia (4); folate supplementation (3); patient education + provide an avoid-list of oxidant drugs/foods and screen family (3); hematology consult, confirm with repeat G6PD assay after recovery (2). PENALTY: continuing or re-prescribing the oxidant drug or giving another oxidant (e.g., nitrofurantoin, dapsone, rasburicase, primaquine) (-3); reflexively starting steroids as if this were autoimmune hemolysis (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: G6PD oxidative hemolysis: immediately discontinue and avoid the offending oxidant drug (here a sulfa antibiotic) and remove other triggers (fava beans, naphthalene). Supportive care: IV hydration to maintain urine output and protect against hemoglobinuria-related renal injury; serial Hgb and renal monitoring. Transfuse packed red cells if anemia is severe or symptomatic. Folate supplementation. Patient education with a written list of oxidant drugs/foods to avoid, and consider screening at-risk family members. Hematology consult; confirm with a repeat G6PD enzyme assay 2–3 months after the acute episode (it can be falsely normal during reticulocytosis). Most episodes are self-limited as older RBCs are cleared.

criticalActions.item: Stop/avoid the offending oxidant drug and counsel on triggers

coreActions.item: Supportive care — hydration to protect kidneys, monitor Hgb/renal function
Patient education with oxidant avoid-list; screen at-risk family

bonusActions.item: Hematology consult; confirm with repeat G6PD assay after recovery

penalties.item: Continuing or re-prescribing the oxidant drug / giving another oxidant

requiredActions: Stop/avoid the offending oxidant drug and counsel on triggers
optionalActions: Supportive care — hydration to protect kidneys, monitor Hgb/renal function
Patient education with oxidant avoid-list; screen at-risk family
Hematology consult; confirm with repeat G6PD assay after recovery
unsafeActions: Continuing or re-prescribing the oxidant drug / giving another oxidant

standardizedPatient.communicationStyle: answers questions asked without volunteering diagnostic clues
standardizedPatient.affect: 28-year-old man of Mediterranean descent, mildly jaundiced and tired. Cooperative, mildly anxious about the dark urine.
disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

triggerConcepts: did you start a new drug
historyTriggers.response: Yes — I just started an antibiotic a couple of days ago for a bladder infection. I think it was a sulfa one.
Now that you mention it, I had some fava beans at a relative's house a few days ago. I usually steer clear of them.
Something like this happened once years ago, milder, after I ate those beans. It went away on its own.
An uncle on my mom's side has some kind of blood problem with certain drugs. My mom said I was yellow as a newborn.
It's dark, almost like cola or strong tea. I don't see actual red blood in it, and it doesn't burn anymore.
It came on fast — over the last two days, right after I started the antibiotic.
I'm short of breath when I move around and a bit lightheaded. Some dull ache in my back. No chest pain.
No, I haven't had any black or bloody stools, no nosebleeds, and I'm not bruising.
No real fevers, no confusion, no bad headaches, and no diarrhea.
Just the occasional drink, nothing heavy.

physicalExamMappings.finding: HEENT: conjunctival pallor consistent with anemia.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur. No focal neuro deficit.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Skin: no petechiae, no purpura, no rash (pertinent negative against TTP/HUS).
Abdomen: soft; spleen tip only minimally palpable / generally not enlarged; no marked tenderness.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 28M of Mediterranean descent presents with two days of fatigue, jaundice, and cola-colored urine after starting a new oxidant drug. The diagnosis is G6PD deficiency with acute oxidative hemolysis, reached by eliciting the trigger history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup (bite cells/Heinz bodies, negative Coombs, hemolytic markers) while excluding the must-not-miss alternative TTP/HUS.
keyHistoryClues: Recent new oxidant drug (sulfa antibiotic) two to three days before symptoms
Fava bean ingestion and prior milder self-limited episode
Mediterranean ancestry, neonatal jaundice, and X-linked family history
Cola-colored urine without frank hematuria (intravascular hemolysis/hemoglobinuria)
keyPhysicalExamFindings: HEENT: conjunctival pallor consistent with anemia.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, soft systolic flow murmur. No focal neuro deficit.
Skin: no petechiae, no purpura, no rash (pertinent negative against TTP/HUS).
Abdomen: soft; spleen tip only minimally palpable / generally not enlarged; no marked tenderness.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, drawing out the oxidant drug/fava bean trigger, ancestry, and family history only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal jaundice, pallor, and the pertinent-negative absence of petechiae/purpura.
Build a differential led by G6PD oxidative hemolysis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss TTP/HUS.
Order a focused workup (CBC + retic, smear for bite cells/Heinz bodies, Coombs, G6PD assay) and interpret EKG/CXR and labs, recognizing G6PD can be falsely normal during the crisis.
Commit to G6PD deficiency hemolysis and manage by stopping the trigger, supportive hydration, selective transfusion, education, and hematology follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Continuing or re-prescribing the oxidant drug / giving another oxidant
Relying on a single normal G6PD level drawn during the acute crisis to exclude the diagnosis
Reflexively starting steroids as if this were autoimmune hemolysis
managementPearls: Stop/avoid the offending oxidant drug and counsel on triggers
Supportive hydration protects against hemoglobinuria-related renal injury
Repeat the G6PD assay 2–3 months after recovery to confirm
criticalWorkupItems: Peripheral smear — bite/blister cells, Heinz bodies vs schistocytes
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia; no acute ischemic changes.
No acute cardiopulmonary process (excludes pneumonia/effusion as a cause of dyspnea).
criticalManagementItems: Stop/avoid the offending oxidant drug and counsel on triggers
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia; no acute ischemic changes.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia; no acute ischemic changes.
imageInterpretationPearls: A near-normal study is an interpretation task in its own right — name what is and is not present, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
